revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tainan Shrine]][[en:Tainan Shrine]][[ja:台南神社]][[simple:Tainan Shrine]][[zh:臺南神社]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11411931]]
Wikidata name: Tainan Shrine
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Taiwan}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| native_name           = 台南神社
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Tainan Shrine.JPG
| deity                 = [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]]
| consecration_year     = 1920
| prefecture= {{ill|Tainan City|qid=Q15912096}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|22.989638888888887|N|120.20269444444445|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] made outside of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]].<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa]].<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the [[:en:Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|Japanese invasion of Taiwan]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from [[:en:Malaria|Malaria]].<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|qq=|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Commons}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tainan Shrine]][[en:Tainan Shrine]][[ja:台南神社]][[simple:Tainan Shrine]][[zh:臺南神社]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11411931]]
Wikidata name: Tainan Shrine
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Taiwan}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| native_name           = 台南神社
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Tainan Shrine.JPG
| deity                 = [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]]
| consecration_year     = 1920
| prefecture= {{ill|Tainan City|WD=Q15912096}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|22.989638888888887|N|120.20269444444445|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] made outside of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]].<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa]].<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the [[:en:Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|Japanese invasion of Taiwan]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from [[:en:Malaria|Malaria]].<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|qq=|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Commons}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Taiwan}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| native_name           = 台南神社
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Tainan Shrine.JPG
| deity                 = [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]]
| consecration_year     = 1920
| prefecture= {{ill|Tainan City|WD=Q15912096}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|22.989638888888887|N|120.20269444444445|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] made outside of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]].<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa]].<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the [[:en:Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|Japanese invasion of Taiwan]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from [[:en:Malaria|Malaria]].<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Commons}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] made outside of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]].<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa]].<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the [[:en:Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|Japanese invasion of Taiwan]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from [[:en:Malaria|Malaria]].<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Commons}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| native_name           = 台南神社
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Tainan Shrine.JPG
| deity                 = [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]]
| consecration_year     = 1920
| prefecture= {{ill|Tainan City|WD=Q15912096}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|22.989638888888887|N|120.20269444444445|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] made outside of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]].<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa]].<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the [[:en:Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|Japanese invasion of Taiwan]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from [[:en:Malaria|Malaria]].<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] made outside of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]].<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa]].<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the [[:en:Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|Japanese invasion of Taiwan]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from [[:en:Malaria|Malaria]].<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Taiwan}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| native_name           = 台南神社
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Tainan Shrine.JPG
| deity                 = [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]]
| consecration_year     = 1920
| prefecture= {{ill|Tainan City|WD=Q15912096}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|22.989638888888887|N|120.20269444444445|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] made outside of [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]].<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa]].<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the [[:en:Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|Japanese invasion of Taiwan]]<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from [[:en:Malaria|Malaria]].<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of [[:en:Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa]] was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of [[:en:Koxinga|Koxinga]], a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Commons}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} made outside of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} by the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan}}.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa}}.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the {{ill|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|en|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|WD=Q717373|lt=Japanese invasion of Taiwan}}<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from {{ill|Malaria|en|Malaria|WD=Q12156|lt=Malaria}}.<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} made outside of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} by the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan}}.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Taiwan}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| native_name           = 台南神社
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Tainan Shrine.JPG
| deity                 = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}}
| consecration_year     = 1920
| prefecture= {{ill|Tainan City|WD=Q15912096}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|22.989638888888887|N|120.20269444444445|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} made outside of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} by the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan}}.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa}}.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the {{ill|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|en|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|WD=Q717373|lt=Japanese invasion of Taiwan}}<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from {{ill|Malaria|en|Malaria|WD=Q12156|lt=Malaria}}.<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Taiwan}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| native_name           = 台南神社
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Tainan Shrine.JPG
| deity                 = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}}
| consecration_year     = 1920
| prefecture= {{ill|Tainan City|WD=Q15912096|1=Tainan City}}
| coordinates= {{Coord|22.989638888888887|N|120.20269444444445|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} made outside of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} by the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}}.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the {{ill|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|en|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|WD=Q717373|lt=Japanese invasion of Taiwan|1=Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)}}<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from {{ill|Malaria|en|Malaria|WD=Q12156|lt=Malaria|1=Malaria}}.<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga|1=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242|1=Kunitama}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Commons}}

'''Tainan Shrine''' ({{Lang-ja|台南神社|translit=tainan jinja}}) was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} made outside of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} by the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}.<ref name=":12">{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London New York (N.Y.) Oxford |pages= |language=English}}</ref>{{rp|101}} It was linked to imperialism and {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} rather than local support for [[Shinto|Shintoism]]<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Nakajima |first=Michio |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>{{rp|30}} It was established in 1920 and upgraded in 1925 and its main deity was {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}}.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}}<ref name=":1" /> who died during the {{ill|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|en|Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)|WD=Q717373|lt=Japanese invasion of Taiwan|1=Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1895)}}<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Operations |first=United States Office of the Chief of Naval |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YOcdAAAAMAAJ&dq=Tainan+jinja&pg=PA61 |title=Taiwan (Formosa).: Tainan Province |date=1944 |publisher=Office of the Chief of Naval Operations, Navy Department |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} from {{ill|Malaria|en|Malaria|WD=Q12156|lt=Malaria|1=Malaria}}.<ref name=":2">{{cite news |author1=Han Cheung |date=26 May 2019 |title=Taiwan in Time: The prince who became a god |work=Taipei Times |url=http://www.taipeitimes.com/News/feat/archives/2019/05/26/2003715796}}</ref><ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}} He was enshrined in most shrines in Taiwan including [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]].<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=台南神社及其外苑（現成功橋）-國立成功大學文學院-踏溯課程 |url=http://exptainan.liberal.ncku.edu.tw/index.php?option=module&lang=cht&task=showlist&id=610&index=1 |trans-title=Flowing Konishi. Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga|1=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}

[[Koxinga Shrine]] was built by the followers of Koxinga and the Japanese converted it into a Shinto Shrine after their invasion. {{ill|Isogai Seizō|ja|磯貝静蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isogai Seizō|1=Isogai Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24840772}} requested it be a national shrine but it ended up only being Ranked quite low as a prefectural shrine.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|108}}

Tainan Shrine was built on the site of the death of the prince, a few blocks away from [[Koxinga Shrine]].<ref name=":12" />{{rp|110}} It was unique in being granted permission to worship only the prince and no other deities, as almost all other shrines would worship the {{Nihongo|Three Pioneer Kami|開拓三神|Kaitaku Sanjin}} {{ill|Ōkunitama|simple|Kunitama Omikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkunitama|qq=|WD=Q9692242|1=Kunitama}}, [[Ōkuninushi]], and [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona]] too..<ref name=":12" />{{rp|112}}

People were forced to visit shrines at this time by the government rather than going of their own volition.<ref name=":0" />{{rp|38}} It held an elaborate festival every January.<ref name=":1" />

The main office is still used today.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-04 |title=Former Tainan Shinto Shrine Office-Taiwan Religious Culture Map-Religious Cultural Heritage in Taiwan |url=https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221004041420/https://www.taiwangods.com/html/Cultural_EN/3_0011.aspx?i=234 |archive-date=2022-10-04 |access-date=2023-04-30}}</ref>

{{Commons}}

Route introduction. Tainan Shrine and its outer garden (currently Successful Bridge)|access-date=2023-04-12 |website=National Cheng Kung University}}</ref> This was seen as a beginning of a new Taiwanese Japanese civilization.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|101}}

The death of {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|en|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|WD=Q3197569|lt=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa}} was presented as parallel to the much older story of the life of {{ill|Koxinga|en|Koxinga|WD=Q197615|lt=Koxinga|1=Koxinga}}, a Japanese man who became an official under the Mind dynasty, and was forced to flee to Taiwan after the Qing took over, drove the Dutch from Taiwan and died of malaria.<ref name=":12" />{{rp|107}}
